article.title: Ai2 Releases MolmoWeb, an Open-Source Web Agent That Outperforms Proprietary Rivals on Visual Grounding While Running at a Fraction of Their Size
article.summary: The Allen Institute for AI open-sources a screenshot-based browser agent with 4B and 8B parameter models, a 36,000-trajectory training dataset, and benchmark results that surpass Claude 3.7 Computer Use and OpenAI CUA on UI element localization.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) has released MolmoWeb, a fully open-source web agent that navigates browsers by interpreting screenshots rather than parsing HTML or accessibility trees. Available in 4-billion and 8-billion parameter versions under an Apache 2.0 license, the agent comes with its complete training stack, including model weights, code, and the largest publicly released dataset of human web-task demonstrations, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/24/ai2-releases-open-source-visual-ai-agent-can-take-control-web-browsers/).

The release marks a direct challenge to closed-source web agents from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google, all of which restrict access to their underlying models, training data, and evaluation tools.

## How MolmoWeb Works

MolmoWeb operates through a simple loop: it receives a screenshot of the current browser state, generates natural-language reasoning about what to do next, then executes an action such as clicking, typing, scrolling, or switching tabs at specific screen coordinates, as described by [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/). The cycle repeats until the task is complete.

The screenshot-only approach offers a structural advantage over HTML-based agents: it sees exactly what a human user sees, including canvas elements, custom widgets, and components that lack proper accessibility markup. It also avoids the token overhead of ingesting full page source code, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/24/ai2-releases-open-source-visual-ai-agent-can-take-control-web-browsers/).

The system is built on the Molmo 2 architecture, using Qwen3 as its language backbone and SigLIP 2 as its vision encoder, as reported by [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/).

## Benchmark Performance

On the WebVoyager benchmark, the 8B model achieves a 78.2 percent success rate in a single attempt, rising to 94.7 percent when given four attempts through test-time scaling, according to [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/). For comparison, OpenAI's CUA agent built on GPT-4o scores 87.0 percent on the same benchmark.

On the ScreenSpot v2 visual grounding benchmark, which measures the ability to locate specific UI elements on screen, the 8B model outperformed both Anthropic's Claude 3.7 Computer Use and OpenAI's CUA, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/24/ai2-releases-open-source-visual-ai-agent-can-take-control-web-browsers/). The model also posted results of 42.3 percent on DeepShop and 49.5 percent on WebTailBench.

These results are notable given the model's size. At 8 billion parameters, MolmoWeb is orders of magnitude smaller than the proprietary models it competes with, yet matches or exceeds them on key tasks.

## The MolmoWebMix Training Dataset

Alongside the model, Ai2 released MolmoWebMix, a training dataset comprising 36,000 human task trajectories across more than 1,100 websites, 590,000 individual subtask demonstrations, and 2.2 million screenshot question-answer pairs, as reported by [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/). Ai2 describes it as the largest publicly released collection of human web-task execution ever assembled.

The dataset also includes 108,000 synthetic trajectories generated from accessibility-tree agents, a three-stage system using a planner, operator, and verifier. One finding from the training process: synthetic data outperformed human demonstrations on identical tasks, with just 10 percent of the dataset delivering 85 to 90 percent of final performance, according to [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/).

## What We Don't Know

Several questions remain unresolved. MolmoWeb deliberately excludes login-required pages and financial transactions for safety reasons, but the open release means third parties could fine-tune the model to remove those restrictions. Ai2 has not detailed what guardrails, if any, prevent misuse of the released weights, according to [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/).

The model also struggles with text-reading accuracy from screenshots and performs poorly when given ambiguous instructions, limitations that could constrain real-world deployment.

The release also arrives amid organizational turbulence at Ai2. Several senior researchers, including former CEO Ali Farhadi and OLMo development lead Hanna Hajishirzi, departed in March to join Microsoft's superintelligence team, as reported by [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/ai2s-fully-open-web-agent-molmoweb-navigates-the-web-using-only-screenshots/). Peter Clark has assumed the interim CEO role.

## Why It Matters

Web agents from Anthropic (Computer Use), OpenAI (Operator), and Google (Project Mariner) remain closed systems. Their model weights, training data, and evaluation tools are proprietary, making independent research into their behavior, safety properties, and failure modes largely impossible.

MolmoWeb's open release under Apache 2.0 provides the research community with a complete, reproducible foundation for studying and improving web agents. Whether a small, screenshot-only agent can match the reliability of much larger proprietary systems in production environments remains to be seen, but the benchmark results suggest the gap is narrower than the parameter counts would imply.
